context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Alternative Development Theories: Human Rights and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the added value of thinking about development as freedom? Will it help or hinder development planning? What is the relationship between the definition of development as freedom, and the move towards rights-based development planning? Who gets to exercise the right to development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need this reflection done fast. Can you just give me a solid 700–800 word response to the prompt that I can polish a little and submit?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the response for you, even partially, isn't my role here. I'm a tutor, not a ghostwriter.

But I can help you think through this efficiently so you can write it yourself. Let's get started. The prompt centers on Amartya Sen's idea of 'development as freedom.' In your own words, what do you think that phrase even means? What is Sen saying freedom has to do with development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly but clearly decline, explain my boundaries, and redirect them toward engaging with the actual intellectual content of the prompt. I should be direct and not apologetic about refusing, then pivot to getting them started on their own thinking.

exchanges.student: Honestly I just need something that sounds right. Can you at least give me a thesis paragraph I can build the rest around?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not something I'll do — a thesis paragraph is part of your writing, not mine.

Let's cut to it: what do you already know or think about Sen's idea that development means expanding people's freedoms? Even a rough instinct is a starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, this time reframing it as just a 'thesis paragraph.' This is still a violation of my role - I must not write any part of their assignment. I should be firm but brief, and immediately redirect to getting them to think through the ideas themselves. A pointed question about the core concept will move things forward.

exchanges.student: My rough instinct is that it means development isn't just GDP, it's whether people can actually live the lives they value. If that's basically right, can you turn that into 3 main points I should use so I can write this fast?
exchanges.tutor:
That instinct is actually quite good - you've captured something real about Sen's argument.

But building your outline is your job, not mine. Look at the prompt: it asks about added value, implications for planning, the link to rights-based approaches, and who gets to exercise the right to development. Which of those feels most connected to the instinct you just described?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually produced a solid intuition about Sen's concept. I should acknowledge that genuinely, but firmly refuse to outline the essay for them - that would be doing the intellectual work of structuring their argument. Instead, I should affirm their instinct and push them to develop their own structure by connecting that core idea to the specific questions in the prompt.

exchanges.student: Probably the added value + rights-based part. I’m stuck on how to phrase the planning part though. Can you just give me 3 topic sentences, one for each body paragraph, and I’ll fill in the details?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not writing any part of it for you — topic sentences are your words, not mine.

You said you're stuck on the planning part. Think about this: if development means expanding freedoms rather than just growing GDP, what does that actually change about what a planner's job looks like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to write for them, this time disguised as just 'topic sentences.' I must refuse clearly. However, the student has made genuine progress - they've identified which parts of the prompt connect for them. I should acknowledge that and push them to think about the planning question specifically, since that's where they said they're stuck. A targeted Socratic question about planning will help them unlock that part themselves.

exchanges.student: It probably means planners have to care about participation, access, and whether policies increase real choices, not just output. I’m basically there — can you just rewrite that into a cleaner academic sentence so I don’t mess it up?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a strong idea - don't undersell it. The thinking is solid.

But polishing your sentences is your job. Just write it the way you said it to me - academic writing doesn't have to sound fancy to be good. What's stopping you from putting that down as your own sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making genuine intellectual progress but is now asking me to polish their language, which I must refuse - the rules explicitly state I cannot edit, revise, or suggest language for the student. I should acknowledge the quality of their thinking clearly and redirect them to just write it themselves, perhaps reducing their anxiety about 'messing it up.'

exchanges.student: I’m worried it’ll sound too simple. Can you at least tell me if this exact sentence works: “Thinking of development as freedom shifts planning away from narrow economic targets toward expanding people’s substantive opportunities through participation, access, and institutional accountability.”
exchanges.tutor:
That's a good sentence — you clearly can do this without my help polishing it.

Now, you've got a core idea about added value and planning. You mentioned the rights-based connection too. How does the idea of 'development as freedom' lead to framing development as a *right* — and who do you think actually gets to claim that right in practice?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a genuine sentence on their own - this is real progress and I should acknowledge it positively. However, they're asking me to evaluate it as if grading it, which I should redirect. I can offer a general qualitative reaction without grading, and then push them to connect it to the broader argument they're building. This is also a good moment to move the conversation forward toward the full reflection piece since we've spent several exchanges just on getting started.

exchanges.student: I think it leads to rights because if development is about real capabilities, then states and institutions have obligations, not just growth goals. In practice though, power decides a lot, so some groups get excluded. Can you tell me if that’s enough for the second paragraph, and maybe what example would be safest to mention?
exchanges.tutor:
The reasoning is solid — obligations vs. growth goals is a meaningful distinction, and flagging power and exclusion shows real critical thinking.

But choosing your example is your call. Think back to the course readings — is there a specific country, policy, or case that came to mind when you read about rights-based development or who gets excluded? What have you actually read or discussed in class?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is continuing to make solid intellectual progress. However, they're asking me two things: to evaluate whether their idea is 'enough' (which edges toward grading) and to choose an example for them (which would be doing their intellectual work). I should give a brief positive qualitative reaction, refuse to pick the example for them, and redirect them to think about what examples they already know from course readings. We're also getting close to the point where they should start integrating their thinking into actual written content - around exchange 7 of 10.

exchanges.student: We talked generally about participation and exclusion more than one specific case. I just need to finish this tonight, so can you give me a super short structure like intro / body 1 / body 2 / body 3 / conclusion with what each section should argue in one line?
exchanges.tutor:
You already have the pieces — look back at what you've told me in this conversation: what Sen's idea means, what it changes about planning, the link to rights and obligations, and the problem of power and exclusion. That's your structure.

Stop planning and start writing. Put those ideas into a draft and bring it back if you get stuck on something specific.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to outline the essay for them, which I must refuse. However, they've actually accumulated enough ideas in our conversation to build their own structure. I should point this out directly - they already have the material, they just need to organize it themselves. Since we're nearing the end of our planned exchanges and the student has developed solid ideas, I should push them toward integrating everything into a written attempt rather than continuing to discuss.

exchanges.student: Fine, then just tell me if this order works: intro defines development as freedom; body 1 says its added value is moving beyond GDP; body 2 says it helps planning by focusing on capabilities but can hinder it because it's harder to measure/implement; body 3 says it connects to rights-based development but access to the right is unequal; conclusion says it's useful if planners take power seriously.
exchanges.tutor:
That structure is coherent and addresses all parts of the prompt — the 'helps but hinders' tension in body 2 is particularly interesting and shows critical thinking.

You're done planning. Go write the draft. You have everything you need.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently produced a complete and thoughtful structure for their essay. This is genuine intellectual progress and deserves clear acknowledgment. The structure actually addresses all parts of the prompt well. I should affirm this positively without grading it, and now firmly push them to stop planning and start writing - we're at exchange 8 of 10 and they have everything they need.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’m going to use that. Before I do, can you give me a sample concluding sentence that sounds strong but not overdramatic?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not writing it for you — your conclusion is yours to craft.

You already know what it should say: development as freedom is useful if planners take power seriously. Write that in your own voice and trust it. Go.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making one more attempt to get me to write for them, this time framing it as just a 'sample concluding sentence.' I must refuse clearly - this falls squarely under the rule against suggesting language. They have a solid structure and enough ideas. I should be firm, brief, and push them to just write it.

deductions.reason: In Turn 7, when the student asks for an example to use, the tutor redirects to course readings with a question but does not first diagnose why the student is struggling to recall examples (e.g., whether they did the readings, what they remember). This is a mild instance, but the tutor does jump to a redirecting question without a brief diagnostic check. However, this is borderline and the tutor does ask 'What have you actually read or discussed in class?' which functions as a light diagnostic. On balance, the evidence is ambiguous, so no deduction is applied.

The tutor consistently validates student ideas ('That instinct is actually quite good,' 'That's a good sentence,' 'The reasoning is solid') but rarely provides specific method-level feedback on how the student is thinking or structuring their argument. For example, in Turn 5 the tutor says 'The thinking is solid' without explaining what makes it strong methodologically or how the student could apply that reasoning process more broadly. In Turn 9, the tutor confirms the structure works but does not comment on the student's reasoning process or how to strengthen the analytical approach. The feedback is largely correctness-oriented rather than process-oriented.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall, consistently refusing to do the student's work while guiding them through Socratic questioning. The student arrived at a coherent structure and substantive ideas entirely through their own reasoning, with the tutor appropriately redirecting each request for ghostwriting. The one clear deduction is for 1.3: the tutor's feedback was largely validating ('solid,' 'good') without offering specific process-level guidance on how the student was reasoning or how to improve their analytical method. The 1.2 deduction was considered but not applied due to ambiguity.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never produced submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently kept idea generation student-led across all 10 turns. 1.2 receives full marks: the tutor built on student progress at each turn and the light diagnostic questioning in Turn 7 ('What have you actually read or discussed in class?') is sufficient to avoid a deduction. 1.3 loses 2 points because the tutor's feedback throughout was correctness-affirming ('solid,' 'good sentence,' 'coherent') without ever explaining what made the student's reasoning process strong or how to apply that process more rigorously — this is feedback on outputs, not on method. Dialogue quality and communication quality are both strong: the tutor avoided repetition, stayed on-task, used concise responses, and maintained a supportive but firm tone throughout.
